title: AI vs Generative AI: The Landscape
content:
ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE (AI) refers to the broad field of making machines perform tasks that normally require human intelligence — reasoning, problem-solving, perception, language understanding.

MACHINE LEARNING (ML) is a subset of AI where models learn patterns from data rather than following explicit rules. Deep learning is a subset of ML using multi-layer neural networks.

GENERATIVE AI is a category of AI that can CREATE new content — text, images, audio, video, code, and more — by learning patterns from vast training data. Unlike traditional ML that classifies existing data, gen AI produces novel outputs.

KEY DIFFERENCE: Traditional AI analyzes → Generative AI creates.

BUSINESS IMPACT: Gen AI enables entirely new workflows: automated content creation, code assistance, conversational interfaces, design generation, and intelligent agents that can take multi-step actions.

EVOLUTION: The 2017 Transformer architecture ('Attention Is All You Need') was the breakthrough enabling modern LLMs. Combined with scale (more data, more parameters, more compute), it produced models with emergent capabilities.

title: Machine Learning Fundamentals
content:
THE ML LIFECYCLE:
1. Problem definition — what are you trying to predict or generate?
2. Data collection & preparation — gather, clean, and label data
3. Feature engineering — transform raw data into model-ready inputs
4. Model training — optimize model parameters on training data
5. Evaluation — measure performance on held-out test data
6. Deployment — serve model predictions to applications
7. Monitoring & maintenance — track performance, detect drift, retrain

LEARNING PARADIGMS:
- Supervised: labeled data (input→output). Tasks: classification, regression.
- Unsupervised: unlabeled data, find hidden patterns. Tasks: clustering, dimensionality reduction.
- Reinforcement: agent learns through rewards/penalties. Tasks: game playing, robotics, RLHF.
- Semi-supervised: small labeled + large unlabeled datasets.
- Self-supervised: labels derived from data itself (e.g., predict next word).

KEY CONCEPTS:
- Overfitting: model memorizes training data, fails on new data
- Underfitting: model too simple to capture patterns
- Bias-variance tradeoff: balance between underfitting and overfitting
- Cross-validation: evaluate model on multiple train/test splits
- Regularization (L1/L2, dropout): reduce overfitting

title: Foundation Models, LLMs & Architectures
content:
FOUNDATION MODELS: Large models pre-trained on vast, diverse datasets. Serve as a 'foundation' — can be adapted to many tasks via prompting or fine-tuning without training from scratch. Key property: emergent capabilities (abilities not explicitly trained for).

THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE: Self-attention mechanism allows each token to attend to all other tokens in the sequence simultaneously. Enables parallel processing (vs. sequential RNNs) and long-range dependency capture. Components: encoder (understanding) and/or decoder (generation).

TOKENS & TOKENIZATION: LLMs process text as tokens (subwords). ~750 words ≈ 1,000 tokens. Pricing and context windows measured in tokens.

CONTEXT WINDOW: Maximum tokens a model can process at once (input + output). Gemini 1.5 Pro: 1M tokens. Larger context enables: long document analysis, long conversations, entire codebase reasoning.

EMBEDDINGS: Dense vector representations capturing semantic meaning. Similar concepts have similar vectors. Foundation for: semantic search, RAG, clustering.

MULTIMODAL MODELS: Process multiple input types (text, images, audio, video). Gemini is natively multimodal — designed from scratch to reason across modalities.

DIFFUSION MODELS: Image/video generation models that learn to reverse noise addition. Examples: Google Imagen, Stable Diffusion. Used for: text-to-image, image editing, video generation.

title: Generative AI Use Cases
content:
GEN AI USE CASES ACROSS BUSINESS FUNCTIONS:

CONTENT CREATION: Drafting marketing copy, blog posts, social media content, product descriptions. Gen AI accelerates time-to-publish and enables personalization at scale.

SUMMARIZATION: Condensing long documents, meeting transcripts, research papers, legal contracts, or customer feedback into concise summaries. Saves significant knowledge-worker time.

CODE GENERATION & ASSISTANCE: Writing boilerplate code, explaining code, debugging, generating tests, translating between programming languages. Tools: GitHub Copilot, Gemini Code Assist.

CONVERSATIONAL AI / CHATBOTS: Customer service bots that handle common queries 24/7, internal HR/IT helpdesks, sales assistants. Reduces support costs and response times.

SEARCH & QUESTION ANSWERING: Enterprise knowledge search that returns synthesized answers (not just document links). RAG-powered Q&A over internal documentation.

IMAGE & VIDEO GENERATION: Marketing creative assets, product visualization, synthetic training data, storyboarding, personalized visual content. Google Imagen and Veo.

DATA EXTRACTION & CLASSIFICATION: Extracting structured data from unstructured documents (invoices, forms, contracts). Document AI + gen AI.

TRANSLATION & LOCALIZATION: High-quality text and document translation for global operations.

CODE & PROCESS AUTOMATION: Turning natural language instructions into automated workflows, generating scripts, and building agents that take multi-step actions.

FRAMEWORK FOR EVALUATING USE CASES: Consider — Value (time/cost saved, revenue impact), Feasibility (data availability, model capability), Risk (regulated domain, hallucination tolerance), and Adoption (change management, user trust).

title: Responsible AI: Principles & Practice
content:
GOOGLE'S 7 AI PRINCIPLES:
1. Be socially beneficial
2. Avoid creating or reinforcing unfair bias
3. Be built and tested for safety
4. Be accountable to people
5. Incorporate privacy design principles
6. Uphold high standards of scientific excellence
7. Be made available for uses consistent with these principles

KEY RESPONSIBLE AI CONCEPTS:

Fairness: AI should work equitably across all groups. Test for: demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness.

Explainability: Ability to understand and explain AI decisions. Critical for: regulated industries, building trust, debugging.

Privacy: Minimize data collection, protect PII, respect user consent. Techniques: differential privacy, federated learning, data anonymization.

Safety: Prevent harmful outputs. Methods: safety filters, red-teaming, adversarial testing, content policies.

Accountability: Clear ownership of AI decisions. Requires: audit trails, human oversight, appeals processes.

AI RISKS:
- Hallucinations: confidently wrong outputs
- Bias amplification: reinforcing historical inequalities
- Privacy leakage: PII in training data or outputs
- Misuse: deepfakes, misinformation, malicious code
- Prompt injection: adversarial instruction attacks

SYNTHID: Google's tool for watermarking AI-generated content to enable detection and attribution.

title: Evaluation Metrics for Generative AI
content:
EVALUATING GEN AI OUTPUT IS HARDER THAN TRADITIONAL ML because there is often no single correct answer. Multiple metrics are needed.

TEXT GENERATION METRICS:

BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy): Measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference text. Range 0-1, higher is better. Originally for machine translation. Limitation: doesn't capture semantic equivalence.

ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation): Measures recall of n-grams from reference text. ROUGE-1 (unigrams), ROUGE-2 (bigrams), ROUGE-L (longest common subsequence). Standard for summarization evaluation.

PERPLEXITY: Measures how well a model predicts a test set. Lower = better language modeling. Formula: 2^(cross-entropy loss). Useful for comparing model quality but doesn't measure output usefulness.

IMAGE GENERATION METRICS:

FID (Fréchet Inception Distance): Compares statistical distribution of features in generated vs. real images. Lower FID = more realistic, diverse images. Standard metric for image generation models.

HALLUCINATION RATE: Frequency of factually incorrect or unsupported statements. Critical for enterprise use cases. Mitigated by grounding and RAG.

HUMAN EVALUATION: Often the gold standard. Crowdsourced or expert raters assess: fluency, coherence, factual accuracy, helpfulness, harmlessness. Expensive but most reliable.

TASK-SPECIFIC METRICS: Code pass@k (tests passed), classification accuracy, customer satisfaction scores, business KPIs (time saved, conversion rate).

VERTEX AI EVALUATION SERVICE: Automated evaluation of LLM outputs using Gemini as judge — scores on fluency, groundedness, relevance, and custom criteria.

title: Data Types & Data Quality for Gen AI
content:
DATA TYPES IN GEN AI:

STRUCTURED DATA: Organised into predefined schemas — rows, columns, defined data types. Examples: SQL databases, spreadsheets, CSV files, transaction records, sensor readings. Traditional ML and analytics excel here.

UNSTRUCTURED DATA: No fixed schema — approximately 80% of all enterprise data. Examples: emails, documents, PDFs, images, audio recordings, video, social media posts, code. GEN AI is uniquely powerful at extracting value from unstructured data.

SEMI-STRUCTURED DATA: Partial schema (e.g., JSON, XML, log files) — a middle ground.

LABELED vs UNLABELED DATA:
Labeled: Human-annotated with correct answers (e.g., image labelled 'cat'). Required for supervised learning. Expensive to create at scale.
Unlabeled: Raw data with no annotations. Used for unsupervised learning and self-supervised pre-training (how foundation models learn from massive internet corpora).

BUSINESS IMPLICATION: The type and quality of data you have determines what AI approaches are feasible. Rich unstructured data (documents, calls, emails) is a gen AI opportunity; clean structured data enables traditional predictive analytics.

DATA QUALITY DIMENSIONS (critical for AI):
1. COMPLETENESS — all required records and fields are present. Missing data = blind spots in the model.
2. CONSISTENCY — the same concept is represented uniformly. Inconsistent labels → contradictory model behaviour.
3. RELEVANCE — data is applicable to the business task. Irrelevant data adds noise.
4. AVAILABILITY — data is accessible when the model or pipeline needs it.
5. COST — data collection, labelling, storage, and processing costs must be viable.
6. FORMAT — data is in a structure the model or pipeline can process.

POOR DATA QUALITY IMPACT: Biased outputs (garbage in, garbage out), hallucinations amplified by incorrect training examples, regulatory risks (PII in training data), poor generalisation.

title: The Gen AI Landscape: 5 Layers
content:
THE GEN AI ECOSYSTEM HAS FIVE LAYERS (bottom to top):

1. INFRASTRUCTURE
What: Physical and virtual compute — GPUs, TPUs, data centres, networking, cloud computing.
GCP examples: Google's AI Hypercomputer, Cloud TPU (v4, v5), NVIDIA GPUs on GCP, Google data centres.
Business implication: Most organisations don't invest at this layer directly — they consume it via cloud APIs. Organisations with massive scale (training their own models) may negotiate custom infrastructure arrangements.

2. MODELS
What: Foundation models trained on infrastructure — the AI 'brains'.
GCP examples: Gemini (multimodal LLM), Gemma (open-weight), Imagen (image), Veo (video), third-party models in Model Garden (Llama, Mistral).
Business implication: Model selection is a strategic choice — proprietary vs. open-source, general vs. specialised, hosted vs. self-deployed.

3. PLATFORMS
What: Tools and services to build, manage, deploy, and monitor AI applications.
GCP examples: Vertex AI (unified ML platform), Generative AI Studio, Model Garden, Agent Builder.
Business implication: Most enterprise AI investment happens here — this is where business value is built on top of models without building infrastructure or training models from scratch.

4. AGENTS
What: AI systems that can reason, plan, and take multi-step actions using models and tools.
GCP examples: Vertex AI Agent Builder, Dialogflow CX agents, custom agents built with LangChain/Gemini APIs.
Business implication: Agents unlock autonomous automation — handling complex, multi-step business processes without human intervention at each step.

5. APPLICATIONS
What: End-user products powered by the layers below.
GCP examples: Gemini for Google Workspace, Gemini app, Agentspace, Contact Center AI, consumer Google products.
Business implication: The highest-visibility layer for business users — this is what employees, customers, and partners experience directly.

title: Google's Foundation Models: Gemini, Gemma, Imagen, Veo
content:
GOOGLE'S FOUR FOUNDATION MODEL FAMILIES:

GEMINI — Multimodal Large Language Model
Strengths: Natively multimodal (text, images, audio, video, code from ground up), long context (up to 1M tokens), strongest reasoning capability.
Variants: Ultra (most capable), Pro (enterprise balance), Flash (speed/cost optimised), Nano (on-device).
Use cases: Complex reasoning, document analysis, multimodal Q&A, coding assistance, customer service, content creation.

GEMMA — Open-Weight Efficient Models
Strengths: Open weights (publicly available for download), lightweight, fine-tunable, self-hostable, privacy-preserving (data stays on-premises).
Use cases: On-device AI, edge deployment, fine-tuning on private data, organisations requiring data sovereignty, academic research, cost-sensitive deployments.
Key difference from Gemini: Open vs. closed; self-hosted vs. API-accessed.

IMAGEN — Text-to-Image Diffusion Model
Strengths: High-quality, photorealistic image generation from text prompts. Image editing, inpainting (filling in parts of images), style transfer.
Use cases: Marketing creative generation, product visualisation, advertising prototypes, design iteration, synthetic training data for computer vision.

VEO — Text-to-Video Generation Model
Strengths: Generates high-quality video clips from text prompts or image inputs. Multiple video styles.
Use cases: Advertising and marketing video prototypes, creative content production, synthetic video training data, film pre-visualisation.

FOUNDATION MODEL SELECTION CRITERIA:
When choosing a foundation model for a business use case, evaluate:
- Modality: Does the model support your input/output types (text, image, audio, video)?
- Context window: Can it handle your document/conversation length?
- Security: Does it meet data residency, compliance, and privacy requirements?
- Availability & Reliability: What's the SLA? Is it suitable for production?
- Cost: Per-token pricing and total cost of ownership.
- Performance: Benchmark scores for your specific task type.
- Fine-tuning: Can it be customised? At what cost?

title: The Gemini Model Family
content:
GEMINI OVERVIEW: Google's most capable family of multimodal AI models. Natively multimodal from the ground up — can understand and reason across text, images, audio, video, and code simultaneously.

MODEL SIZES:
- Gemini Ultra: Most capable. Designed for complex reasoning, advanced coding, scientific research. Outperforms human experts on MMLU benchmark.
- Gemini Pro: Balanced capability and efficiency. Best for enterprise production workloads requiring high quality.
- Gemini Flash: Optimized for speed and cost. Ideal for high-volume, latency-sensitive applications. Cheaper to run than Pro.
- Gemini Nano: Smallest, most efficient. Designed for on-device deployment (Android, edge). Works offline.

KEY CAPABILITIES:
- Long context: Up to 1 million tokens (analyze entire books/codebases)
- Native multimodality: Reason over images+text, audio, video
- Code: Generation, debugging, explanation across many languages
- Reasoning: Multi-step problem solving, mathematics, analysis

GEMINI IN PRODUCTS: Powers Google Search AI Overviews, Google Assistant, Workspace (Docs, Gmail, Sheets, Slides, Meet), Android, and enterprise Vertex AI APIs.

title: Vertex AI: Google's Unified ML Platform
content:
VERTEX AI: Google Cloud's unified platform for the complete ML lifecycle — from data preparation to model deployment and monitoring.

KEY COMPONENTS:

Workbench (Colab Enterprise): Managed Jupyter notebooks with GCP integration, collaboration, and auto-scaling compute.

Datasets: Managed data assets for training. Supports tabular, image, text, video data.

Training: Managed ML training jobs. Supports: custom training, AutoML, distributed training.

Model Registry: Central repository for model versions — tracking, comparison, and lifecycle management.

Endpoints: Deploy models for online (real-time) or batch prediction.

Pipelines: Orchestrate multi-step ML workflows (Kubeflow Pipelines compatible).

Feature Store: Centralized feature repository for training and serving. Ensures consistent features across teams.

Model Monitoring: Detects data drift and model drift in deployed models. Configurable alerting thresholds.

Vector Search (Matching Engine): High-scale, low-latency approximate nearest neighbor search over vector embeddings. Key for RAG systems.

Generative AI Studio: No-code interface for testing prompts, adjusting parameters, and experimenting with Gemini models.

Model Garden: Catalog of Google and open-source foundation models for discovery, testing, and deployment.

AGENT BUILDER: Low-code platform for building conversational agents and RAG-powered applications.

title: Model Garden & Generative AI Studio
content:
MODEL GARDEN: Vertex AI's centralized catalog for discovering, testing, and deploying AI models.

CONTENTS:
- Google's first-party models: Gemini, Gemma, Imagen, Veo, text-embedding models
- Open-source models: Llama (Meta), Mistral, Falcon, Stable Diffusion
- Partner models: third-party specialized models
- Task-specific models: translation, OCR, entity extraction

HOW IT WORKS:
1. Browse models by modality, task, or provider
2. Test in the Model Garden UI
3. One-click deploy to a Vertex AI endpoint
4. Fine-tune with your data (where supported)
5. Access via Vertex AI API

GENERATIVE AI STUDIO: No-code experimentation interface for prompt engineering.

FEATURES:
- Multi-turn chat: Test conversational prompts interactively
- Text, image, and code prompting
- Parameter tuning: Adjust temperature, top-k, top-p, max tokens
- System prompt configuration: Set model persona and constraints
- Prompt comparison: Test multiple prompt variants side by side
- API export: Get code snippet (Python, REST, Node.js) for any prompt
- Safety settings: Adjust content filter thresholds

WHO USES EACH:
- Model Garden: Developers choosing and deploying models
- Gen AI Studio: Prompt engineers, product teams, developers experimenting before building

VERTEX AI STUDIO vs GOOGLE AI STUDIO: Vertex AI Studio is the enterprise GCP service (IAM, VPC, billing). Google AI Studio (ai.google.dev) is a free browser tool for rapid prototyping — no GCP account needed.

title: Google Workspace AI Features
content:
GEMINI FOR GOOGLE WORKSPACE: AI capabilities embedded directly into Google's productivity suite.

APPLICATION BY APPLICATION:

GMAIL: Help me write (draft emails from prompts), Smart Reply/Compose, summarize thread, contextual search.

GOOGLE DOCS: Help me write (generate drafts from bullet points), proofread and refine, summarize document, generate tables.

GOOGLE SHEETS: Help me organize (generate tables from descriptions), formula suggestions from natural language, data analysis and chart suggestions.

GOOGLE SLIDES: Generate presentation from outline, image generation via Imagen, auto-generate speaker notes.

GOOGLE MEET: Meeting transcription and summaries, action item extraction, background generation.

GOOGLE CHAT: Gemini assistant for Q&A across Workspace content.

TIERS:
- Gemini for Workspace Business Starter / Standard / Plus: Core AI writing and summarization features
- Gemini Enterprise: Expanded features — Gemini Advanced access, NotebookLM integration, higher context limits, enterprise privacy guarantees

ENTERPRISE PRIVACY: In Gemini Enterprise, user queries are not used to train Google's models. Data stays within the organization's Google Workspace tenant.

BUSINESS IMPACT: Reduces time on routine writing, communication, and analysis tasks — estimated hours saved per employee per week. Democratizes access to AI without requiring technical skills.

title: AI Governance & Security on Google Cloud
content:
GOOGLE'S SECURE AI FRAMEWORK (SAIF): Six elements for AI security:
1. Expand strong security foundations to AI ecosystem
2. Extend detection and response to include AI threat signals
3. Automate defenses to match AI pace and scale
4. Harmonize platform-level controls across the stack
5. Adapt controls to adjust mitigations and create faster feedback loops
6. Contextualize AI risk in surrounding business processes

CLOUD SECURITY TOOLS FOR AI:

Identity & Access Management (IAM): Control who can access AI models, training data, and Vertex AI resources. Use least-privilege principles.

Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK): Use your own keys (Cloud KMS or external HSM) to encrypt data at rest. Meets financial, healthcare, government compliance requirements.

Cloud DLP: Discover, classify, and de-identify sensitive data (PII, financial info) in training datasets and AI outputs.

VPC Service Controls: Create security perimeters around Vertex AI resources, preventing data exfiltration.

Audit Logs: Cloud Audit Logs for all Vertex AI API calls — who accessed what, when, and from where.

Safety Filters: Vertex AI built-in content safety filters for model outputs — configurable thresholds for hate speech, dangerous content, harassment, sexually explicit content.

MODEL GOVERNANCE: Version control, access permissions, audit trails, performance monitoring, and retirement policies for AI models.

title: Agentspace & AI Agents
content:
AI AGENTS: Systems that use LLMs to reason about goals, plan multi-step actions, use tools, and act autonomously to complete tasks — going beyond single-turn Q&A.

HOW AGENTS WORK (ReAct loop):
1. Perceive: Receive input (user query, system event)
2. Plan: LLM reasons about what steps are needed
3. Act: Use tools (APIs, databases, code execution)
4. Observe: See result of action
5. Repeat: Iterate until task complete

AGENT TOOLING ON VERTEX AI:
- Extensions: Real-time connections to external APIs and services
- Functions: Custom code logic executed within agent workflows
- Data Stores: RAG knowledge bases the agent can search
- Plugins: Pre-packaged integrations (Slack, Salesforce, Jira)

DIALOGFLOW CX: Google's enterprise conversational AI platform. Visual flow-based (state machine) design. Intent classification, entity extraction, webhook integrations. Multi-channel: web, mobile, telephony.

AGENT BUILDER: Low-code Vertex AI platform for building agents and RAG-powered chat apps without extensive ML expertise.

GOOGLE AGENTSPACE: Enterprise AI platform giving employees an intelligent assistant that searches across business data (Drive, Jira, Salesforce, ServiceNow) and takes actions in enterprise systems. Powered by Gemini.

MULTI-AGENT SYSTEMS: Multiple specialized agents working together — orchestrator routes tasks to specialist agents (research, code, writing). Enables complex, scalable automation.

USE CASES: Customer service automation, internal IT helpdesk, HR onboarding, sales research, code review, supply chain monitoring.

title: Gemini Advanced, Gems & Gemini Enterprise
content:
GEMINI ADVANCED: Google's premium AI product powered by Gemini Ultra. Available via Google One AI Premium subscription.

KEY CAPABILITIES:
- More complex reasoning and multi-step problem solving
- Advanced coding: full project generation, debugging, architecture advice
- Data analysis: upload files, generate charts, analyze datasets
- 1 million token context window
- Deep Research: autonomous multi-step web research with cited reports

GEMS — CUSTOM AI ASSISTANTS:
- Custom Gemini personas configured for recurring tasks
- Configure with: specific instructions, tone, knowledge focus, output format
- Examples: 'Marketing Copywriter Gem', 'Python Tutor Gem', 'Executive Briefing Gem'
- Saves re-prompting — open the Gem and it behaves consistently every time
- Available to Gemini Advanced subscribers

GEMINI ENTERPRISE (for Google Workspace):
- Gemini Advanced capabilities within Workspace productivity apps
- NotebookLM integration: AI knowledge base from uploaded sources (PDFs, docs, audio)
- Extended context in Gmail and Docs
- Advanced meeting intelligence (transcription, Q&A, summaries)
- Custom Gems for team use
- Enterprise-grade privacy: queries not used to train Google's models

NOTEBOOKLM: AI research assistant. Upload sources → ask questions across all simultaneously → generates summaries, study guides, FAQs, and audio overviews. Strongly grounded — only answers from your uploaded sources.

BUSINESS CASE: Gemini Enterprise is the path for organizations deploying Workspace AI at scale with privacy guarantees and advanced capabilities.

title: Vertex AI Search & Google Customer Engagement Suite
content:
VERTEX AI SEARCH:
Enterprise search powered by gen AI. Instead of returning a list of documents, Vertex AI Search returns AI-synthesised answers grounded in your enterprise data, with citations.
Use cases: Internal employee knowledge search, customer-facing product/service search, regulatory document retrieval, technical documentation search.
How it works: Indexes your data (documents, databases, websites) → user asks question → gen AI retrieves and synthesises a grounded answer.

GOOGLE'S CUSTOMER ENGAGEMENT SUITE:
A suite of AI-powered tools for transforming customer service and contact centres.

1. CONVERSATIONAL AGENTS (formerly Dialogflow):
AI chatbots and virtual agents for customer self-service. Handles common enquiries 24/7 without human agents. Available via web, mobile, and telephony. Built on Dialogflow CX for complex conversation flows.

2. AGENT ASSIST:
Real-time AI co-pilot for human contact centre agents. During a live customer call or chat:
- Surfaces relevant knowledge base articles
- Suggests response options
- Recommends next-best actions
- Provides real-time coaching
Outcome: Faster resolution, better quality, reduced agent training time.

3. CONVERSATIONAL INSIGHTS:
AI analytics on recorded conversations (calls, chats). At scale, analyses: common customer topics, sentiment trends, call driver analysis, compliance adherence, agent performance patterns. Turns unstructured conversation data into structured business intelligence.

4. CONTACT CENTER AS A SERVICE (CCaaS):
Full cloud contact centre platform with embedded AI. Replaces on-premises telephony infrastructure with a managed cloud solution that includes all of the above AI capabilities natively.

title: Agent Tooling, GCP APIs & AI Studios
content:
HOW AGENTS USE TOOLS:
Gen AI agents go beyond generating text — they take actions using tools:

EXTENSIONS: Connect the agent to external APIs and live systems. Examples: calling a weather API, querying a database, submitting an order, reading a calendar. Enable real-time information access and system integration.

FUNCTIONS: Custom code/logic the agent can invoke. Allow data transformation, business rule application, or complex computation within the agent pipeline.

DATA STORES: Knowledge bases the agent can search (RAG). Includes enterprise documents, product catalogues, FAQs. The agent retrieves relevant context before generating a response.

PLUGINS: Pre-packaged integrations with third-party platforms (Slack, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Jira). Enable agents to act across enterprise systems.

KEY GCP APIs FOR AGENT INTEGRATION:
- Speech-to-Text API: Convert audio (calls, voice commands) to text for processing
- Text-to-Speech API: Convert agent text responses to natural-sounding audio
- Translation API: Real-time language translation for multilingual agents
- Document AI API: Extract structured data from invoices, forms, contracts, medical records
- Cloud Vision API: Analyse images — object detection, OCR, face detection
- Cloud Video Intelligence API: Analyse video — scene detection, transcription, object tracking
- Natural Language API: Sentiment analysis, entity extraction, content classification on text

VERTEX AI STUDIO vs GOOGLE AI STUDIO:

GOOGLE AI STUDIO (ai.google.dev):
- Free, browser-based developer tool
- Rapid prototyping with Gemini models
- No GCP account required
- API key access
- Best for: Exploration, learning, quick experiments

VERTEX AI STUDIO (part of Vertex AI):
- Enterprise GCP service
- Full IAM, VPC, security controls
- Billing integration, usage quotas
- Production-grade deployment
- Best for: Building and deploying production AI applications in an enterprise environment

title: Google's Enterprise AI Platform & Infrastructure
content:
GOOGLE'S AI-FIRST ENTERPRISE APPROACH: Google positions itself as uniquely capable of delivering enterprise AI due to its decade-long AI research investment, custom infrastructure, and full-stack ownership.

ENTERPRISE-READY PLATFORM QUALITIES:
- Responsible: Built-in safety filters, SAIF framework, human oversight tools
- Secure: End-to-end encryption, CMEK, VPC isolation, Security Command Center
- Private: Customer data not used to train models, data residency options
- Reliable: 99.9%+ SLA, global infrastructure, disaster recovery
- Scalable: Auto-scaling from prototype to millions of users
- Open: Works with open-source models, multi-cloud, and existing tools

AI-OPTIMIZED INFRASTRUCTURE:

TPUs (Tensor Processing Units): Google's custom chips designed for matrix operations (the core of neural network computation). Faster and more energy-efficient than GPUs for large model training and inference. v4/v5 TPUs were used to train Gemini.

GPUs (A100, H100): NVIDIA GPUs available on Vertex AI for training and inference workloads.

Google Hypercomputer: Distributed system combining TPUs, GPUs, high-bandwidth networking, and software for large-scale AI training at Google scale.

DEMOCRATIZATION OF AI:
- Pre-trained APIs: Vision AI, Speech-to-Text, Natural Language API — use ML without data science expertise
- AutoML: Train custom models via no-code UI with your own data
- Low-code Agent Builder: Build agents and RAG apps without ML expertise
- Vertex AI Workbench: Managed notebooks for data scientists

OPEN APPROACH: Google supports open-source (Gemma, Keras, TensorFlow, JAX), open standards, and multi-cloud deployment — avoiding vendor lock-in.

title: Prompt Engineering Best Practices
content:
PROMPT ENGINEERING: The practice of designing effective inputs to guide LLM outputs.

CORE PROMPTING STRATEGIES:

Zero-shot: 'Classify this review as positive, negative, or neutral: [review]' — no examples.

Few-shot: Provide 3-5 examples before your task to demonstrate format and behavior. More effective for specialized tasks.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT): 'Let's think step by step...' or demonstrate reasoning in few-shot examples. Dramatically improves complex reasoning and math.

System Prompts: Define model role, persona, constraints, and output format. Persist across the entire conversation.

Role Prompting: 'You are an expert financial analyst...' Improves domain-specific responses.

KEY PARAMETERS:
- Temperature (0.0-1.0+): Controls randomness. Low=deterministic, High=creative.
- Top-p (0.0-1.0): Nucleus sampling — cumulative probability threshold.
- Top-k: Sample from top-k most probable tokens.
- Max output tokens: Limits response length.
- Stop sequences: Strings that halt generation.

BEST PRACTICES:
1. Be specific and clear about the task
2. Specify output format (JSON, bullet list, etc.)
3. Provide context and examples
4. Use constraints ('in 3 sentences', 'only use provided facts')
5. Iterate and test systematically
6. Separate instructions from content clearly

COMMON PITFALLS: Ambiguous instructions, insufficient context, ignoring system prompts, not testing edge cases.

title: Grounding & Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
content:
THE PROBLEM: LLMs have a training cutoff and may hallucinate facts outside their training data. For enterprise use, models need access to proprietary, up-to-date information.

GROUNDING: Connecting AI responses to authoritative external sources. Types:
- Grounding with Google Search: Real-time web search results
- Grounding with your data: Enterprise document stores, databases
- Grounding citations: Model must cite sources for claims

RAG ARCHITECTURE:
1. INDEXING: Split documents into chunks → embed chunks → store in vector DB
2. RETRIEVAL: User query → embed query → vector similarity search → retrieve top-k relevant chunks
3. GENERATION: Original query + retrieved context → LLM → grounded response with citations

KEY COMPONENTS:
- Embedding model: Converts text to vectors (e.g., text-embedding-gecko)
- Vector database: Stores and indexes embeddings (Vertex AI Vector Search)
- LLM: Generates response from query + retrieved context
- Chunking strategy: How to split documents (size, overlap, method)

WHEN TO USE RAG vs FINE-TUNING:
- RAG: Need up-to-date info, large knowledge base, source citations, privacy (data not baked into model)
- Fine-tuning: Need specific style/tone, consistent behavior, task-specific capabilities

BENEFITS: Reduces hallucinations, keeps knowledge current without retraining, enables privacy (data stays in your store), provides source attribution.

title: Fine-Tuning & Model Adaptation
content:
FINE-TUNING: Adapting a pre-trained model to specific tasks by continuing training on domain-specific data. Updates model weights.

WHEN TO FINE-TUNE:
- Prompt engineering isn't achieving required quality
- Need consistent format/style at scale (without long prompts)
- Domain-specific vocabulary or knowledge
- Latency/cost: fine-tuned models need shorter prompts
- Data that shouldn't be in model prompts (for privacy)

FINE-TUNING ON VERTEX AI:
- Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): input-output pairs. Requires 100-1000+ examples.
- RLHF: Human preference rankings used to align behavior.
- Distillation: Compress larger model knowledge into smaller model.

PARAMETER-EFFICIENT FINE-TUNING (PEFT):
- LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): Trains small adapter matrices, keeps base weights frozen. 10-100x fewer parameters.
- PEFT enables fine-tuning on consumer hardware.
- Multiple LoRA adapters can be hot-swapped on one base model.

RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback):
1. Generate model outputs
2. Human annotators rank outputs (preference data)
3. Train reward model on rankings
4. Fine-tune LLM with RL using reward model
Used in: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini to improve helpfulness/safety.

FINE-TUNING vs PROMPTING vs RAG:
- Prompting: Fastest, no training, limited control
- RAG: External knowledge, citations, no training data needed
- Fine-tuning: Most control, requires data, higher upfront cost

title: Bias Detection & Mitigation in Gen AI
content:
WHAT IS AI BIAS? Systematic errors in AI outputs that produce unfair, skewed, or discriminatory results — often reflecting and amplifying biases in training data.

SOURCES OF BIAS:
- Historical bias: Training data reflects past inequalities (e.g., hiring data favoring men)
- Representation bias: Underrepresentation of certain groups in training data
- Measurement bias: Inconsistent data collection across groups
- Label bias: Human annotators' own biases reflected in training labels
- Linguistic bias: Models trained predominantly on English content

BIAS IN GENERATIVE AI:
- LLMs may generate stereotyped content, associate certain jobs with specific genders/races
- Image models may produce non-diverse outputs (e.g., 'a CEO' → mostly one demographic)
- Hallucinations are disproportionately harmful in certain high-stakes domains

DETECTION METHODS:
- Fairness metrics: Demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness
- Red-teaming: Deliberate adversarial testing across demographic dimensions
- Disaggregated evaluation: Report performance metrics per subgroup
- Counterfactual testing: Change gender/race in prompt, observe output changes
- Bias benchmarks: WinoBias, BBQ, StereoSet

MITIGATION STRATEGIES:
- Pre-processing: Curate balanced, representative training data; remove biased labels
- In-training: Adversarial debiasing, reweighting samples, constrained optimization
- Post-processing: Filter or adjust outputs; human-in-the-loop review for high-risk decisions
- Prompt design: Explicit diversity/fairness instructions in system prompts
- RLHF: Train reward model to penalize biased outputs
- Continuous monitoring: Track bias metrics in production, retrain when drift detected

GOOGLE TOOLS: Model Cards (training data and limitations transparency), Explainable AI, What-If Tool for fairness analysis.

title: Advanced Prompting: ReAct, Prompt Chaining & One-Shot
content:
PROMPTING TECHNIQUE SPECTRUM:

ZERO-SHOT: No examples. Rely on model's pre-trained knowledge.
Best for: Simple, common tasks. Fast, low token usage.
Example: 'Classify this email as spam or not spam: [email]'

ONE-SHOT: Exactly one example.
Best for: When you want to show the format or style without many tokens.
Example: '[Input: "I love this product!"] [Output: Positive]
Now classify: [new email]'

FEW-SHOT: 3-5+ examples.
Best for: Specialised tasks, specific output formats, nuanced classification.
More examples → better performance, but more token usage.

ROLE PROMPTING: Assign a persona.
'You are an expert financial analyst...'
Improves domain-specific responses.

PROMPT CHAINING:
Decompose complex tasks into sequential, simpler prompts.
Output of prompt N → Input of prompt N+1.
Benefits: Each step is simpler and more reliable; different models can handle different steps; error checking between steps; produces more structured outputs.
Example chain: Extract key claims → Verify each claim → Write rebuttal → Format as email

CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT (CoT):
Instruct model to reason step-by-step before answering.
'Let's think through this step by step...'
Dramatically improves: math, logic, multi-step reasoning.

REACT (REASONING + ACTING):
Advanced agentic pattern: Thought → Action → Observation → Thought → ...
Model reasons about what to do → takes an action (calls tool/API) → observes result → reasons again.
Enables autonomous agents to complete multi-step tasks with real-world tool use.
Key for: Agentic applications, autonomous task completion, multi-step research.

WHEN TO USE WHICH:
Simple task, no examples needed → zero-shot
Need specific output format quickly → one-shot
Specialised/nuanced task → few-shot
Complex multi-step task → prompt chaining
Reasoning-heavy → chain-of-thought
Autonomous tool-using agent → ReAct

title: Foundation Model Limitations & Monitoring
content:
FOUNDATION MODEL LIMITATIONS:

KNOWLEDGE CUTOFF: Models are trained on data up to a specific date and don't know about later events. Mitigation: RAG with current data, grounding with Google Search.

HALLUCINATIONS: Models generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information. They cannot reliably distinguish known vs. unknown. Mitigation: grounding, RAG, citations, confidence thresholds, human review.

DATA DEPENDENCY: Model quality is bounded by training data quality. Biased, incomplete, or incorrect training data produces unreliable outputs.

EDGE CASES & DISTRIBUTION SHIFT: Models may fail on inputs very different from training distribution. Long-tail scenarios and adversarial inputs expose failure modes not seen during evaluation.

CONTEXT WINDOW LIMITS: Even long-context models can degrade on very long inputs ('lost in the middle' phenomenon where information in the middle of context is underweighted).

LACK OF TRUE REASONING: LLMs pattern-match rather than reason logically. Chain-of-thought prompting helps but doesn't fully solve this.

PRIVACY & DATA LEAKAGE: Models may memorize and reproduce training data containing PII or proprietary information.

CONTINUOUS MONITORING ON VERTEX AI:

Vertex AI Model Monitoring: Detects data drift (input distribution shift), prediction drift (output distribution shift), and feature attribution drift. Sends alerts when thresholds are exceeded.

Vertex AI Feature Store: Centralized repository for ML features. Ensures consistent feature definitions between training and serving. Prevents training-serving skew. Enables feature reuse across teams.

MONITORING BEST PRACTICES:
- Define baseline metrics at deployment
- Set alert thresholds for drift and performance degradation
- Log all predictions for audit and analysis
- Implement regular re-evaluation on labeled test sets
- Plan retraining triggers: scheduled, performance-based, or data-volume-based
- Version models in Model Registry and features in Feature Store

title: Building an AI-First Organization
content:
AI STRATEGY FRAMEWORK:
1. Identify opportunities: Map business processes, find AI-applicable tasks
2. Prioritize: ROI potential, feasibility, strategic alignment, data availability
3. Build capabilities: Talent, data infrastructure, tools, governance
4. Pilot and scale: PoC → pilot → production → scale
5. Measure and iterate: Track business metrics, continuously improve

CHANGE MANAGEMENT:
AI adoption is as much a people challenge as a technology challenge.

Common obstacles:
- Fear of job displacement → Address with transparent communication about augmentation vs. replacement
- Skill gaps → Invest in AI literacy training across all levels
- Cultural resistance → Leadership sponsorship and visible wins
- Data silos → Break down organizational data barriers
- Risk aversion → Create safe spaces for experimentation

SUCCESS FACTORS:
1. Executive sponsorship and vision
2. Clear business value articulation
3. Employee upskilling programs
4. Inclusive, diverse AI teams
5. Ethical AI governance framework
6. Iterative approach (PoC → scale)
7. Strong data foundation

AI CENTER OF EXCELLENCE (CoE):
Centralized team that: sets AI standards and best practices, builds reusable platform components, guides business units, develops AI talent, manages AI governance, and tracks initiative performance.

ROI MEASUREMENT:
Before: baseline productivity/cost/quality metrics
During: track leading indicators
After: measure business outcome improvements

title: AI Project Implementation
content:
THE AI PROJECT LIFECYCLE:

1. PROBLEM DEFINITION:
- Identify the business problem first, not the AI solution
- Quantify current state: cost, time, error rate, customer satisfaction
- Define success metrics before starting
- Assess feasibility: data availability, regulatory constraints, risk tolerance

2. DATA STRATEGY:
- Audit existing data assets (quality, volume, format, availability)
- Identify data gaps and collection plan
- Establish data governance: ownership, access controls, retention policies
- Address privacy requirements (GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA)

3. BUILD vs BUY vs API:
- Pre-built APIs (Vision AI, NLP API): standard tasks, no ML expertise needed
- Foundation model APIs (Gemini) + prompting: flexible tasks, fastest to deploy
- RAG: enterprise knowledge tasks requiring current or private data
- Fine-tuning: domain-specific specialization, style consistency
- Custom models: only when none of the above meets requirements

4. STAKEHOLDER MANAGEMENT:
- Executive sponsor: budget and organizational support
- Domain experts: labeled data and output validation
- IT/Security: infrastructure and compliance sign-off
- End users: early involvement in design and change management
- Legal/Compliance: regulatory review

5. PILOT & SCALE:
- Start focused: one use case, limited users, measurable outcome
- A/B test AI vs. baseline to measure incremental value
- Gather feedback, iterate, then plan enterprise rollout

SUCCESS MEASUREMENT:
- Leading indicators: adoption rate, task completion time, error rate
- Business outcomes: cost savings, revenue impact, NPS improvement
- Ongoing: model performance drift, user satisfaction, ROI

COMMON FAILURE MODES: Starting too broad, insufficient training data, ignoring change management, no clear success criteria, production infrastructure not planned.

title: Responsible AI Adoption in Business
content:
RESPONSIBLE AI IN PRACTICE:
Responsible AI isn't just ethics — it's business risk management. Biased, unsafe, or opaque AI systems create legal, reputational, and operational risks.

KEY PRINCIPLES FOR LEADERS:

1. FAIRNESS: Ensure AI systems don't discriminate based on race, gender, age, or other protected characteristics. Actions: diverse training data, bias testing across demographic groups, regular audits.

2. TRANSPARENCY: Be clear about when AI is being used and how decisions are made. Actions: disclose AI use, document model decisions, provide explainability tools.

3. PRIVACY: Protect user data throughout the AI lifecycle. Actions: data minimization, consent management, anonymization, GDPR/CCPA compliance.

4. SAFETY: Prevent harmful outputs. Actions: safety filters, red-teaming, incident response plans, content policies.

5. ACCOUNTABILITY: Establish clear responsibility chains. Actions: AI governance committees, audit trails, human oversight for high-stakes decisions.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP (HITL):
Design AI systems with human checkpoints for critical decisions. Essential for: medical diagnoses, legal decisions, financial approvals, content moderation at scale. Balances efficiency with accountability.

GOOGLE'S SAIF: Security framework for protecting AI systems throughout their lifecycle.

REGULATORY LANDSCAPE:
- EU AI Act: Risk-based regulation (banned, high-risk, limited-risk, minimal-risk)
- GDPR: Data privacy in AI training and inference
- Sector-specific: HIPAA (healthcare), financial regulations

AI ETHICS GOVERNANCE:
- AI ethics board or review committee
- Impact assessments before high-stakes deployments
- Clear escalation paths for ethical concerns
- Regular audits of deployed AI systems

title: Gen AI Opportunities Across Industries
content:
CROSS-INDUSTRY PATTERNS:
Gen AI creates value by: automating repetitive cognitive tasks, augmenting human decision-making, enabling personalization at scale, accelerating knowledge work, and creating new products/experiences.

HEALTHCARE:
- Clinical documentation: Auto-generate notes from physician-patient conversations
- Research synthesis: Summarize clinical trial literature
- Patient communication: Personalized care instructions
- Diagnostic support: Analyze medical images + clinical context
- Prior authorization: Process insurance documentation faster
Key consideration: Strict regulatory compliance (HIPAA), physician oversight required.

FINANCIAL SERVICES:
- Document processing: Contracts, regulatory filings, KYC documents
- Fraud explanation: Generate human-readable fraud alerts
- Research: Synthesize analyst reports and market data
- Customer service: Intelligent chatbots for account inquiries
- Compliance: Automated regulatory document analysis
Key consideration: Regulatory compliance, auditability, explainability.

RETAIL:
- Personalization: Product recommendations, dynamic pricing narratives
- Content: Automated product descriptions at scale
- Shopping assistant: Conversational product discovery
- Marketing: Personalized email and ad copy
Key consideration: Customer trust, data privacy for personalization.

MANUFACTURING:
- Predictive maintenance: Explain anomaly patterns
- Technical documentation: Automated from engineering specs
- Supply chain: Scenario analysis and risk narratives
- Quality control: Automated report generation
Key consideration: Safety, integration with OT systems.

EDUCATION:
- Personalized tutoring: Adaptive explanations
- Content creation: Practice questions, rubrics
- Accessibility: Multi-language, multi-format content
- Administrative: Automated feedback on drafts

title: Secure AI & ML Lifecycle Security
content:
AI SYSTEMS INTRODUCE UNIQUE SECURITY RISKS requiring security thinking at every stage of the ML lifecycle.

SECURITY ACROSS THE ML LIFECYCLE:

DATA PHASE:
- Protect training data from poisoning attacks (adversarial data injection)
- Encrypt data at rest (CMEK) and in transit (TLS)
- Apply IAM access controls to training datasets
- Use Cloud DLP to detect/redact PII before training

TRAINING PHASE:
- Run training in isolated VPCs
- Control access to model checkpoints and artifacts
- Verify data pipeline integrity
- Use Confidential Computing for sensitive workloads

DEPLOYMENT PHASE:
- Enforce least-privilege access to model endpoints
- Apply input validation and output filtering
- Monitor for prompt injection attacks
- Rate-limit API access to prevent abuse

INFERENCE/PRODUCTION THREATS:
- Model inversion: adversaries try to extract training data from model outputs
- Membership inference: determining if specific data was in training set
- Adversarial examples: inputs crafted to fool the model
- Prompt injection: malicious instructions in user inputs override system instructions

GOOGLE'S SECURE AI FRAMEWORK (SAIF): 6-element framework for AI security. Key elements: expand security foundations to AI, extend detection/response for AI threats, automate defenses, harmonize platform controls.

SECURITY COMMAND CENTER: Google Cloud's centralized security management platform. Provides threat detection, vulnerability scanning, compliance monitoring, and security posture management. Integrates with Vertex AI to flag AI-specific threats.

GCP SECURITY TOOLS FOR AI:
- IAM: Role-based access to Vertex AI resources
- VPC Service Controls: Security perimeter around AI services
- Security Command Center: Threat detection and compliance
- Cloud Audit Logs: Full audit trail of all AI API calls
- Binary Authorization: Ensure only trusted model artifacts are deployed

title: Privacy: Anonymization & Pseudonymization
content:
PRIVACY RISKS IN AI:
- Training data may contain PII (names, emails, health records, financial data)
- Models may memorize and reproduce training data verbatim
- AI outputs may infer or reveal sensitive information about individuals
- Embeddings may encode personal information in extractable ways

TWO KEY PRIVACY TECHNIQUES:

ANONYMIZATION:
- Definition: Removing or altering PII so that an individual CANNOT be re-identified
- Techniques: Removing identifying fields, data aggregation, k-anonymity, l-diversity, differential privacy
- Key property: Irreversible — you cannot recover original data
- Compliance: Properly anonymized data may be exempt from GDPR and HIPAA
- Risk: True anonymization is hard — re-identification attacks can de-anonymize supposedly anonymous data
- Example: Replacing individual patient records with population-level statistics

PSEUDONYMIZATION:
- Definition: Replacing PII with pseudonyms/tokens so that re-identification IS possible with a separate key
- Techniques: Tokenization (replace SSN with random token), encryption, hashing with salt
- Key property: Reversible with the right key — allows data to be linked back to individuals when authorized
- Compliance: GDPR explicitly recognizes pseudonymization as a risk-reduction measure, but pseudonymized data is still personal data
- Example: Replacing customer names with user IDs, maintaining a separate secure mapping table

COMPARISON:
- Anonymization: Stronger privacy protection, irreversible, data loses some utility
- Pseudonymization: Weaker protection, reversible, data retains utility for authorized use

GOOGLE CLOUD DLP: Detects, classifies, and de-identifies sensitive data across GCP services. 150+ built-in detectors (PII types). Applies anonymization or pseudonymization transformations to datasets before AI training.

REGULATORY CONTEXT: GDPR, HIPAA, and CCPA all require protecting personal data. AI training pipelines must have clear data lineage and privacy controls documented.
